6: egerthe, ouk estin ode — he has been raised; he is not here — the passive egerthe (he was raised, divine passive) identifies the resurrection as God's act, not a resuscitation or self-resurrection. The empty tomb is the negative evidence; the positive evidence is the proclamation (proagei hymas eis ten Galilaian = he is going before you to Galilee). Mark's original ending (assuming 16:8 is the original ending) is characteristically abrupt: the women fled and said nothing to anyone, for they were afraid. The reader is left with the open question that the entire Gospel posed — who do you say that he is? The silence of the women is the silence into which the reader must speak.
1: diagenoumenou tou sabbatou / ēgorasan arōmata / aleiposin auton — the Sabbath having passed / bought spices / to anoint him — diagenoumenou (having passed completely — aorist of diaginomai; the Sabbath is over at sunset Saturday). Three women are named: Mary Magdalene, Mary of James, and Salome (consistent with the witnesses at the cross, 15:40). Arōmata (spices, aromatics) for anointing — they had not been able to complete the burial preparation before the Sabbath; the earlier anointing at Bethany (14:8) was anticipatory burial preparation. Aleiposin auton (to anoint him) — the same verb used at Bethany (14:3 cognate).
2: lian prōi / tē mia tōn sabbatōn / anateilantos tou hēliou — very early / on the first of the week / the sun having risen — lian prōi (extremely early, deep early morning). Tē mia tōn sabbatōn (on the first of the Sabbaths / first day of the week) — the Aramaic/Jewish counting of days; mia (one/first) refers to Sunday. Anateilantos tou hēliou (the sun having risen — genitive absolute) — the precise temporal marker. The darkness of death (the three hours of darkness, 15:33) is over; the first light of the new day frames the discovery.
3: tis apokylisei hēmin ton lithon — who will roll away the stone for us — apokyliō (to roll away — a compound verb; apo- = away + kyliō = to roll). The practical concern as they approach — they have the spices but not the means to open the tomb. The question is never answered: when they arrive, the answer has already been given by an agent greater than any human helper. The stone becomes in Markan theology a marker of divine action.
4: anakekylismenonl / megas sphodra — had been rolled back / extremely large — anakekylismenon (had been rolled back — perfect passive of anakylioō; the perfect tense indicates a completed action with present state: it was rolled and remains rolled back). Megas sphodra (very great/large) — the parenthetical note about the stone's size explains why their concern (v.3) was legitimate and why the discovery is unexpected. No human agent is identified as having moved it.
5: neaniskon / stolichn leukēn / exethambethesan — a young man / white robe — they were greatly alarmed — neaniskos (young man) — this is the third neaniskos in Mark: the unnamed figure of 14:51–52, the young man of 10:20 (variant), and now this figure at the tomb. The white robe (stolē leukē) distinguishes him from the one in the linen cloth: white garments are angelic vestments (Dan 7:9; Rev 7:9). Exethambethesan (were greatly astonished/alarmed — the strongest compound of thambos used in Mark).
7: kai tō Petrō / proagei hymas eis tēn Galilaian / hōs eipen hymin — and to Peter / he is going before you to Galilee / just as he told you — kai tō Petrō (and to Peter) — Peter's specific naming alongside the disciples is remarkable. He had denied Jesus three times (14:66–72); the specific inclusion of his name in the resurrection message is the re-inclusion of the one who most publicly failed. Proagei (is going before — present tense, ongoing action; the risen Lord goes ahead as a shepherd) echoes 14:28 exactly. Hōs eipen hymin (just as he told you) — the fulfillment of the word spoken before the Passion.
8: ephugon / tromos kai ekstasis / ouden ouden eipon / ephobounto gar — they fled / trembling and amazement / they said nothing to anyone / for they were afraid — the final verse of the original Gospel (in the best manuscripts). Tromos (trembling — physical trembling of awe and fear) and ekstasis (ecstasy/astonishment — standing outside oneself; the word for trance or profound disorientation before the divine). Ouden ouden eipon (they said nothing to anyone — double ouden, emphatic). The final clause ephobounto gar (for they were afraid) ends with a gar (for) — an unusual and perhaps unprecedented way to end a sentence or a book. Scholars debate whether this was Mark's intended ending (fear as the proper response to the divine encounter) or whether the original ending was lost; the manuscript evidence supports v.8 as the earliest recoverable ending.
9:
Note on the longer ending (vv.9–20): Verses 9–20 are absent from the two oldest and most important manuscripts (Codex Sinaiticus and Codex Vaticanus) and from the oldest Syriac, Coptic, and Armenian witnesses. Many other manuscripts note its absence or mark it as suspect. The vocabulary and style differ from Mark 1–16:8. Most modern text critics regard it as a later addition (perhaps 2nd century) summarizing resurrection appearances from the other Gospels. Its contents are canonical in the church, but its Markan authorship is doubtful.

anastas de prōi / ephainē prōton Mariai tē Magdalēnē — rising early / he appeared first to Mary Magdalene — ephainē (appeared — aorist passive of phainō; also used for resurrection appearances in Matt 28:7). The longer ending begins by identifying the resurrection and the first appearance, filling out what the original ending left unstated.
10: ekeinē poretheisa / tois met' autou genomenois / penthounsin kai klaiousin — she having gone / to those who had been with him / mourning and weeping — Mary goes to those who had been with Jesus (tois met' autou genomenois) — a roundabout description of the disciples. Penthounsin kai klaiousin (mourning and weeping — the vocabulary of funeral grief): the disciples are in the posture of bereavement when the resurrection news arrives.
11: hoi akousantes hoti zē kai etheathē / ēpistēsan — those hearing that he was alive and had been seen / disbelieved — zē (is alive — present tense) and etheathē (had been seen — aorist passive of theaomai). Ēpistēsan (disbelieved — aorist of apisteō; the negative of pisteuō, to believe). The theme of disbelief at resurrection testimony runs through the longer ending (vv.11, 13, 14) and parallels Luke's Emmaus narrative.
12: en hetera morphē / hois ek tōn duo bainousin — in a different form / to two of them as they walked — hetera morphē (different/other form — morphē is the same word as in Phil 2:6–7 for Christ's pre-incarnate and incarnate forms). The reference parallels Luke's Emmaus road account (Luke 24:13–35), though hetera morphē is a unique addition suggesting a changed or transformed appearance.
13: oud' ekeinois episteusan — they did not believe them either — the triple disbelief structure (vv.11, 13, 14) before the commission (vv.15–18) follows a pattern: persistent unbelief is overcome only by direct encounter with the risen Lord. The emphasis on the disciples' unbelief at the resurrection is consistent with Mark's characteristic portrayal of their failure to understand throughout the Gospel.
14: tois hendeka / ōneidisei tēn apistian autōn kai sklērokardian — to the Eleven / rebuked their unbelief and hardness of heart — hoi hendeka (the Eleven — the formal designation after Judas's defection). Ōneidisen (rebuked, upbraided — a strong verb of reproach). Apistia (unbelief) and sklērokardia (hardness of heart — the word used for divorce in 10:5 and for the Pharisees' hardness in 3:5) are the two sins addressed. Tois theaasamenois auton egēgermenon (those who had seen him raised) — the disciples disbelieved eyewitness testimony.
15: poreuthentes eis ton kosmon hapanta / kēruxate to euangelion pasē tē ktisei — going into all the world / proclaim the gospel to all creation — hapanta ton kosmon (all the world — the universal scope). Pasē tē ktisei (to all creation — the most expansive possible framing; ktisis is creation itself, not merely humanity). The commission frames the Gospel's proclamation as cosmically comprehensive, consistent with the longer ending's escalating universalism.
16: pisteuō kai baptistheis sōthēsetai / ho de apistēsas katakrthēsetai — the one believing and having been baptized will be saved / the one disbelieving will be condemned — the pairing of pisteuō (faith) and baptistheis (baptism) as conditions of salvation links the two sacramental acts. Notably the negative: ho de apistēsas katakrithēsetai (the one not believing will be condemned) — only unbelief (not unbaptized status) is the basis of condemnation, suggesting faith is the central requirement.
17: sēmeia parakolouthēsei tois pisteuousin — signs will follow those who believe — parakolouthēo (to follow alongside, accompany — a word for consistent consequence rather than isolated miracle). The signs listed: casting out demons (daimonia ekbalousin), speaking in new tongues (glōssais lalēsousin kainais), picking up serpents (opheis arousin) — these paralleled the Acts 2 and Acts 28 experiences of the early church.
18: opheis arousin / ean ti thansimon piōsin / ou mē autous blapsē / ep' arrōstous cheiras epithēsousin — they will take up snakes / if they drink anything deadly / it will not harm them / they will lay hands on the sick — the list of signs expands on v.17. The handling of snakes echoes Acts 28:3–6 (Paul and the viper at Malta). Ean ti thansimon piōsin (if they drink anything deadly — conditional) — presented as a protective sign, not a command; it became the basis for some later snake-handling sects. Kalōs hexousin (they will be well/recover).
19: anelēphthē / ekathisen ek dexiōn tou theou — was taken up / sat at the right hand of God — anelēphthē (was taken up — the same verb as Acts 1:11 for the Ascension; also the verb used for Elijah's translation in 2 Kings 2:9–11 LXX). Ekathisen ek dexiōn tou theou (sat at the right hand of God) — the fulfillment of Psalm 110:1 which Jesus cited in 12:36 (ek dexiōn mou kathō) and his own testimony in 14:62 (ek dexiōn... tēs dynameōs). The longer ending's conclusion thus fulfills Jesus's own predictions.
20: ekeinoi de exelthontes ekēruxan pantachou / tou kyriou synergountos kai ton logon bebaiountos — and they going out proclaimed everywhere / the Lord working with them and confirming the word — ekeinoi de (and they — the Eleven) go out and preach everywhere (pantachou). Tou kyriou synergountos (the Lord working together with them — genitive absolute; synergos = co-worker; God as the disciples' fellow-worker). Bebaiountos ton logon (confirming the word — from bebaioō, to make firm, ratify). The longer ending closes with the divine-human synergy of mission: human proclamation, divine confirmation through signs.
